text.title: negotiation Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for negotiation. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Separate the people from the problem: attack the issue, not the person, and allow the counterparty to maintain dignity throughout.
rule.rationale: Attacking the person activates ego defense, which closes down creative solution space. Separating people from problem keeps both parties focused on the shared problem to solve.

rule.statement: Focus on interests, not positions: ask "Why is that important to you?" behind every stated demand to uncover the real need.
rule.rationale: Positions are often incompatible; the interests behind them are usually compatible. A "non-negotiable deadline" (position) may mask "I need predictability for downstream planning" (interest) — which can be met with milestone check-ins rather than a fixed date.

rule.statement: Generate multiple options for mutual gain before selecting a solution — brainstorm without commitment, evaluate after.
rule.rationale: Separating invention from decision prevents anchoring on the first reasonable option, which is rarely the best option for either party.

rule.statement: Use objective criteria (market rates, industry standards, precedent) to evaluate options — never "because I want it".
rule.rationale: Objective criteria depersonalize disagreement: neither party is wrong, the criterion is the arbiter. This preserves relationship while enabling firm positions.

rule.statement: Determine your BATNA (Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement) before every negotiation — this sets your walk-away point and eliminates desperation signals.
rule.rationale: Negotiators with no BATNA reveal desperation through concession speed and body language. A known walk-away point makes every offer evaluable against a real alternative.

rule.statement: Never reveal a weak BATNA — only disclose your BATNA when it is genuinely strong and will improve your position.
rule.rationale: Revealing a weak BATNA destroys leverage and invites the counterparty to push harder, knowing you have no exit.

rule.statement: Map the Zone of Possible Agreement before negotiating: if your walk-away and the counterparty's walk-away do not overlap, no deal is reachable and alternatives must be generated first.
rule.rationale: Entering a negotiation where ZOPA does not exist wastes both parties' time and damages relationship. Identify the overlap — or the absence of one — before the meeting.

rule.statement: Apply at most two Cialdini principles per message — stacking all six reads as a sales script and triggers skepticism.
rule.rationale: Each principle works because it operates below conscious evaluation. Stacking them makes the pattern obvious, which activates resistance rather than compliance.

rule.statement: Anchor first with a specific number, not a range — ranges anchor on the favorable end for the counterparty.
rule.rationale: First numbers set the psychological reference point for the entire negotiation. Ranges like "$80K-$100K" anchor at $80K in the buyer's mind.

rule.statement: After making an offer, stop talking — silence makes the counterparty fill the void, often with a concession.
rule.rationale: Discomfort with silence causes most negotiators to soften their position before they need to. Holding silence is leverage that costs nothing.

rule.statement: Use the nibble (asking for small extras after the main agreement) only after the main agreement is signed, not before.
rule.rationale: Pre-agreement nibbles signal that you are still bargaining on the main deal, which reopens closed points. Post-agreement nibbles are small enough that the counterparty's sunk cost prevents reopening.

rule.statement: Respond to "Take it or leave it" by exploring the constraint behind the position, not by accepting or walking out.
rule.rationale: "Take it or leave it" is almost always a posture, not a real constraint. Exploring what created the constraint often surfaces a creative option neither party had considered.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop before sending: any agent-drafted offer, counter, or persuasion message must be reviewed by the human before delivery — tone and relationship context cannot be fully captured in a brief.
rule.rationale: The agent's BATNA and ZOPA analysis is only as accurate as the inputs; garbage in results in plausible-sounding but wrong brief out.

rule.statement: Simulated counterparties are too rational: real negotiators use emotion, ego, and politics — agent roleplay understates these factors.
rule.rationale: Contract language suggestions from the agent are starting points, not final clauses; flag this explicitly in any output that includes contract terms.

rule.statement: The agent cannot measure which Cialdini principle will be effective for a specific individual; A/B testing is required for persuasion copy.
rule.rationale: Mechanical application of Cialdini tactics in copy can read as manipulative if the underlying offer is weak.
